text: The study of isolated transition metal clusters is an important area of research in surface and catalytic science because these species serve as simple analogues of complex surfaces and nanoparticle catalysts. Structural information regarding these species has largely been obtained via mass-selective infrared action spectroscopy, a technique that bridges the low sample density gap between structural probe techniques and essentially amounts to a search for the vibrational frequencies associated with those changes in the cluster spectrum that result from the dissociation of the cluster or changes in the ionization efficiency. With the availability of intense, broadly tunable infrared free-electron lasers, along with high-pressure cluster sources and enhanced ion traps, it is currently possible to study the binding and activation of small molecules to a wide variety of cluster sizes and charge states. This article reviews the present understanding of the binding of $H_{2}$, $CO$, $N_{2}$, and $O_{2}$ to transition metal clusters, obtained from infrared action spectroscopy and supported by quantum chemical calculations. Comparison of the measured vibrational frequencies with the theoretical spectra allow one to determine whether a species is molecularly or dissociatively adsorbed, and provides detailed information regarding the site of binding (atop, bridging or hollow sites) as well as the changes in the electronic structure of the cluster resulting from binding. Some information regarding the effects of secondary species on the binding of molecules to transition metal clusters, as well as the dynamics of the IR-induced chemistry occurring within the cluster species, is also discussed.
